claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Deployments compare the desired Pod template with existing ReplicaSets, create a new ReplicaSet, and scale old and new replicas according to maxSurge and maxUnavailable.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 1 / first paragraph
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Deployment concept page directly documents rolling updates, ReplicaSets, maxSurge, and maxUnavailable.

claims.claim_text: Argo Rollouts can expose a small traffic slice to a new version, pause, run automated analysis, promote when healthy, and abort when unhealthy.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters / paragraph 3
claims.rationale: The canary strategy docs cover traffic weights and pauses, while the shared analysis source covers automated analysis and abort behavior.

claims.claim_text: A Rollout is mostly compatible with a Deployment but adds extra strategy fields for advanced rollout control.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters / paragraph 4
claims.rationale: The architecture page explicitly describes Rollout as a custom resource mostly compatible with Deployment but with extra rollout-control fields.

claims.claim_text: Argo Rollouts supports two main progressive-delivery patterns: canary and blue-green.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 1 / paragraph before canary and blue-green diagrams
claims.rationale: The shared Rollouts analysis source covers both rollout types, and the blue-green strategy page documents the second pattern explicitly.

claims.claim_text: The cluster controller is the required runtime component; it reconciles Rollout behavior and without it the custom resources do not produce actual rollout actions.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 2 / paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The architecture page defines the controller as the main component that monitors Rollouts and brings cluster state in line with the rollout definition.

claims.claim_text: The kubectl plugin is optional, distinct from the controller, and provides visualization plus operational commands for Rollouts, Experiments, and Analysis resources.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 2 / paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The kubectl-plugin overview says the plugin is optional and is used to visualize and manage Rollouts, Experiments, and Analysis resources.

claims.claim_text: The standard installation path uses the install.yaml manifest, and on macOS Homebrew the plugin package name is kubectl-argo-rollouts.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 2 / installation paragraphs before command blocks
claims.rationale: The installation page gives the exact install.yaml controller command and the Brew package name for the kubectl plugin.

claims.claim_text: Without traffic management, Argo Rollouts makes a best-effort approximation of setWeight by scaling stable and canary ReplicaSets rather than by exact request-percentage routing.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 3 / paragraph after first canary commands
claims.rationale: The canary strategy docs explicitly say that without traffic management the rollout makes a best-effort attempt to achieve the percentage using ReplicaSet scaling.

claims.claim_text: A pause step with a duration resumes after the specified wait, while an empty pause waits indefinitely until promotion.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 3 / paragraph beginning 'A pause step is a deliberate stop'
claims.rationale: The canary docs define pause duration behavior and state that pause steps without duration wait indefinitely until promoted.

claims.claim_text: Traffic-routed canaries use stableService and canaryService, and the NGINX integration uses stableIngress plus optional annotationPrefix while requiring the primary Ingress to target the stable Service.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 4 / paragraph introducing the NGINX example
claims.rationale: The NGINX traffic-management docs describe stableService, canaryService, stableIngress, annotationPrefix, and the requirement that the primary Ingress route to the stable Service.

claims.claim_text: Blue-green rollouts use activeService and optional previewService, and with autoPromotionEnabled set to false user traffic remains on the old version until promotion.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 4 / blue-green explanation and autoPromotionEnabled checkpoint
claims.rationale: The blue-green docs define activeService and previewService and state that autoPromotionEnabled false pauses before promotion instead of switching active traffic automatically.

claims.claim_text: scaleDownDelaySeconds delays scaling down the old ReplicaSet after the active Service switches so service-selector changes can propagate and rollback remains possible briefly.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 4 / paragraph beginning 'The scaleDownDelaySeconds field is more than cleanup timing'
claims.rationale: The blue-green docs explain that scaleDownDelaySeconds exists to allow service-selector propagation before old pods are removed.

claims.claim_text: Blue-green supports prePromotionAnalysis before the traffic switch and postPromotionAnalysis after the switch, with failed post-promotion analysis able to abort and return traffic to the previous stable ReplicaSet.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 4 / paragraph after prePromotionAnalysis and postPromotionAnalysis YAML
claims.rationale: The blue-green docs explicitly define prePromotionAnalysis and postPromotionAnalysis and describe their blocking and rollback effects.

claims.claim_text: AnalysisTemplate defines how to perform canary analysis, while AnalysisRun is the instantiated run whose successful, failed, or inconclusive result affects whether a rollout continues, aborts, or pauses.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 5 / opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The shared analysis source directly defines AnalysisTemplate and AnalysisRun and explains how their outcomes affect rollout progression.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes Job can be used as an analysis metric, and it is considered successful when the Job completes with exit code zero.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 5 / paragraph beginning 'The simplest useful AnalysisTemplate can run a Kubernetes Job'
claims.rationale: The Job metric-provider docs state that Job-backed analysis succeeds when the Job completes and exits with code zero.

claims.claim_text: Background analysis can run while a canary progresses through steps, can be delayed with startingStep, and a failed analysis can abort the rollout and leave it degraded.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 5 / paragraph beginning 'Background analysis starts separately'
claims.rationale: The analysis docs explicitly cover background analysis, startingStep, and failed-analysis abort behavior.

claims.claim_text: For classic Prometheus histograms, histogram_quantile requires the le label to be preserved in the aggregation by clause.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 5 / paragraph beginning 'Latency analysis is similar'
claims.rationale: The Prometheus query-functions docs state that the le label must be included in the by clause for histogram_quantile on classic histograms.

claims.claim_text: Dry-run analysis executes analysis metrics and reports what would have happened without failing the rollout.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 5 / paragraph beginning 'Dry-run analysis is useful during adoption'
claims.rationale: The shared analysis source documents dry-run metrics and shows that failed dry-run metrics do not fail the analysis run.

claims.claim_text: Argo Rollouts notifications use annotations in the form notifications.argoproj.io/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>, and built-in triggers include rollout completion, analysis-run failure, and rollout-step completion.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 6 / paragraph beginning 'Notifications help connect rollout state'
claims.rationale: The notifications docs define the annotation format and list the built-in trigger names used in the module.

claims.claim_text: An experiment step can create temporary baseline and canary ReplicaSets, run analysis, block rollout progress until the experiment succeeds, and abort the rollout if the experiment fails or errors.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Section 6 / paragraph beginning 'Experiments are useful'
claims.rationale: The experiment docs explicitly describe rollout experiment steps as blocking and state that failed or errored experiments abort the rollout.

claims.claim_text: Argo Rollouts does not replace Argo CD: Argo CD syncs desired manifests into the cluster, while Argo Rollouts controls how a changed workload progresses after it is applied.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / bullet 1
claims.rationale: The Argo CD architecture source covers sync and reconciliation, while the shared Rollouts analysis source covers rollout progression after manifests change.

claims.claim_text: AnalysisRuns are Kubernetes custom resources, and job-backed analysis gives operators normal Kubernetes objects and logs to inspect.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / bullet 3
claims.rationale: The shared analysis source defines AnalysisRun as a custom resource, and the Job provider docs show that Job-backed analysis runs as Kubernetes Jobs.

claims.claim_text: Blue-green promotion changes traffic by changing Service targeting, and the old ReplicaSet can remain available briefly for rollback before scale-down policy removes it.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / bullet 4
claims.rationale: The blue-green docs explain that the controller changes Service selectors for promotion and delays scaling down the old ReplicaSet.

claims.claim_text: A Rollout can use pod-ratio traffic before adding ingress or mesh routing, but exact percentage enforcement usually requires a traffic manager.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / bullet 2
claims.rationale: The canary docs state that without traffic management percentages are only approximated via pod scaling and recommend traffic management for finer-grained control.

further_reading.title: Argo Rollouts Analysis and Progressive Delivery
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the best single page for AnalysisTemplate, AnalysisRun, background analysis, dry-run, and rollout-abort behavior.

further_reading.title: Argo Rollouts Canary Deployment Strategy
further_reading.why_relevant: It covers setWeight, pause semantics, pod-ratio behavior, background analysis, and canary traffic-routing fields.

further_reading.title: Argo Rollouts Blue-Green Deployment Strategy
further_reading.why_relevant: It documents active and preview Services, promotion flow, analysis hooks, and scaleDownDelaySeconds.

notes: Per seed rules, quiz items, exercise steps, code blocks, and tables were excluded. The opening marketplace story and the numeric blast-radius worked example read as pedagogical illustration rather than claims about a named real-world incident, so no war-story entry was emitted for them.
